reasoning: The 30-day average of fatalities over the 360 days preceding the forecast date was extremely low, roughly 0.167, given the reference value of 1.167 (1 + 0.167). Since we are checking if a 30-day window will exceed 10 times this reference value (11.67), the threshold is strictly 12 or more fatalities in a single month. Historically in ACLED data for Paraguay, months with 12+ fatalities are extremely rare, happening perhaps once every 3-5 years (e.g., the Tacumbú prison operation in December 2023, the San Pedro riot in 2019, or the Curuguaty clash in 2012). This establishes a base rate of roughly 1-3%. My queries of recent English and Spanish news covering April and May 2025 confirmed that no such mass casualty event has occurred recently, driving the objective probability for the nearest window (May 18) close to zero. Given the strict instruction to increase uncertainty with the time horizon and to keep probabilities bounded at 0.05 or higher, I have anchored the immediate forecast at the lower bound of 0.05 and incrementally scaled it up slightly for the later dates, while maintaining that a YES resolution remains highly unlikely without an unexpected, rare black-swan event (like another massive gang shootout or prison riot).
